name: H4350
notes: The single most-used precision rifle powder in North America. Annual PRS Series end-of-season equipment surveys consistently rank H4350 as the dominant 6.5 Creedmoor and 6mm Creedmoor powder among top-100 finishers, frequently cited at >40% share. Hodgdon Extreme line, temperature-stable.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual 1st Ed online supplement 2024

name: Varget
notes: Workhorse mid-burn-rate stick powder; the canonical .308 Win match powder for decades and the dominant heavy-bullet .223 Rem powder among service-rifle and PRS Gas-Gun shooters. Hodgdon Extreme, temperature-stable.

name: Reloder 16
notes: TZ (temperature-stable) Bofors Nitrochemie powder; the top H4350 alternative cited in PRS Series surveys. Often cross-shopped with H4350 for 6.5 Creedmoor/6mm Creedmoor when H4350 is unavailable. Slightly faster burn than H4350.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: H1000
notes: Slow magnum-rifle powder; a top choice for 7mm and .300 magnums in long-range / NRL-Hunter / ELR competition. Hodgdon Extreme, temperature-stable. Frequently the dominant powder in 7mm PRC and .300 PRC builds.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: Reloder 26
notes: Slow, high-energy temperature-stable powder; widely used by long-range hunters and ELR competitors for max-MV magnum loads. Often paired against H1000 for 7mm/.30-cal magnums; produces noticeably higher MVs at the cost of pressure margin.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: H4831SC
notes: Short-cut version of H4831; the canonical hunting-rifle slow powder for .270 Win and .30-06 class cartridges. Drops more uniformly through powder measures than the long-grain original. Hodgdon Extreme, temperature-stable.

name: N150
notes: Mid-burn Finnish stick powder; widely used in European F-Class and benchrest disciplines, increasingly common in North American precision .308 builds. Reputation for low ES/SD with appropriate primer choice.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: N570
notes: Very slow magnum powder, a primary competitor to Reloder 33 for ELR / .338 Lapua / .300 Norma Mag. Featured prominently in modern long-range / King of 2 Mile loads.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024
King of 2 Mile match equipment notes 2023-2024

name: H4895
notes: Faster-burning member of the Hodgdon Extreme family; reduced-load friendly (the Hodgdon 'Youth Loads' program uses it down to 60% of max). Strong .308 / .30-06 traditional hunting choice.

name: Retumbo
notes: Among the slowest Hodgdon Extreme powders. Heavily used in big-case magnums and ELR; known as a top-3 .338 Lapua Mag powder. Temperature-stable.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: IMR 4350
notes: Classic medium-slow stick powder; the original .30-06 / .270 Win standard before Hodgdon Extreme launched. Still widely loaded by traditional hunting reloaders. Less temperature-stable than H4350.

name: IMR 4064
notes: Mid-burn stick powder; long-time match-rifle .308 Win choice (Sierra MatchKing data) before Varget rose to prominence. Still widely used in Service Rifle and CMP competition.

name: IMR 4166
notes: First IMR Enduron series powder; sold as a temperature-stable Varget alternative with copper-fouling reducer. Strong following among reloaders who couldn't get Varget during the 2020-2022 shortage.

name: H4198
notes: Fast rifle stick powder; heavily used in 6PPC benchrest, .223 Rem light-bullet varmint loads, and a primary subsonic .300 Blackout choice.

name: H322
notes: The historic 6PPC benchrest powder; still widely used in short-range benchrest where it shares the throne with N133. Hodgdon Extreme.

name: N133
notes: The benchrest world's reference 6PPC powder; arguably the single most-used powder in short-range group benchrest (IBS / NBRSA). Used worldwide for that discipline.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: N140
notes: Faster than N150; the European analog to Varget for many cartridges. Common in F-Class and 300m ISSF.

name: N160
notes: Slightly slower than N150; a frequent .30-06 / 6.5x55 choice in Europe. Used by long-range hunters as an H4350-class option.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: N165
notes: Slow rifle stick powder; covers the .300 Win Mag and 7mm Rem Mag space for European reloaders.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: N170
notes: Slowest of the traditional N100-series; comparable to H1000. Used in big magnums where slower-burn temperature-stable powders are needed.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: Reloder 17
notes: Bofors-made faster Reloder series powder; popular in mid-case 6.5mm and 7mm cartridges. Less temperature-stable than RL16/RL26.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: Reloder 22
notes: Slow magnum stick powder; long-time .270 Win / 7mm Rem Mag standard, especially among traditional hunters.

name: Reloder 23
notes: TZ (temperature-stable) sibling to RL22. Marketed for sniper/match use. Solid .300 Win Mag option for cold-weather hunting.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: Reloder 25
notes: Slow magnum powder; a primary .338 Lapua / .300 Wby Mag choice for hunters and ELR shooters before Reloder 33 launched.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: Reloder 33
notes: Very slow rifle powder; designed specifically for the .338 Lapua Mag and similar military / ELR cases. King of 2 Mile and Ko2M-class events feature this powder heavily.

name: H50BMG
notes: Hodgdon Extreme line slow powder developed for .50 BMG match loads. The standard .50 BMG match powder. Used in some ELR rifle programs as well.
sources: Fifty Caliber Shooters Association equipment notes

name: IMR 4895
notes: Original WW2-era stick powder, still currently shipping. Common surplus and traditional reloader choice. Less temp-stable than H4895; otherwise comparable.

name: IMR 4198
notes: Fast stick powder; main competitor to H4198 in the .223 Rem light-bullet and subsonic .300 BLK roles.

name: IMR 7828 SSC
notes: Short-cut version of IMR 7828; slow rifle powder for big-bore magnums. Flows uniformly through measures.

name: IMR 4451
notes: Enduron-series powder positioned as a temperature-stable IMR 4350 / H4350 alternative. Adoption growing among reloaders affected by H4350 shortages.

name: IMR 7977
notes: Slowest IMR Enduron; a temperature-stable competitor to H1000 / Retumbo. Newer release with growing adoption.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: IMR 8133
notes: Very slowest Enduron-series powder; a Retumbo / H50BMG-class option. Adopted by ELR shooters with the largest cases.

name: A2520
notes: Western Powders extruded mid-burn analog of Varget / IMR 4064. Often sold under the Accurate brand for service-rifle reloaders.

name: A2700
notes: Mid-slow ball powder in the H4350 burn class. Western Powders / Accurate brand offering.

name: A4350
notes: Western Powders mid-slow extruded; an H4350-class alternative branded under Accurate. Reloader's pick when H4350 is unavailable.

name: MAGPRO
notes: Western Powders / Accurate slow ball powder, marketed for short magnums. Strong following among WSM family reloaders.

name: LT-32
notes: Lapua Test 32; Lovex-made fine-grain extruded powder developed for the 6BR / Dasher benchrest scene. Has displaced N133 for some 6BR shooters.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: TAC
notes: Western Powders / Ramshot ball powder positioned for AR-15 / .223 Rem service rifle. Known for clean burning and uniform metering. Common AR-loader's pick.

name: BIG GAME
notes: Ramshot mid-burn ball powder in the IMR 4350 / H4350 class for hunting cartridges. Western Powders/Hodgdon catalog.

name: MAGNUM
notes: Ramshot slow ball powder for magnum cartridges. Less common than Reloder 22/26 and H1000 but used by ball-powder fans.

name: LRT
notes: Ramshot Long Range Tactical; slow temperature-stable ball powder developed for sniper / ELR applications.

name: URP
notes: Universal Rifle Powder; medium-slow Norma stick powder roughly comparable to H4350 / N160. Common in European hunting reloads.

name: MRP
notes: Magnum Rifle Powder; Norma's signature slow magnum extruded powder. Long history with European hunters; comparable to H4831.

name: 203-B
notes: Mid-burn Norma stick powder used widely in 300m ISSF and European F-Class. Comparable to N150 / Varget.

name: N555
notes: Newer Vihtavuori 500-series high-energy mid-burn powder; positioned for modern PRS-style 6mm/6.5mm cartridges.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: N565
notes: Vihtavuori 500-series slow high-energy powder. Aimed at high-MV mid-magnum loads competing with Reloder 26.
sources: Berger Bullets Reloading Manual online supplement 2024

name: N568
notes: Newest Vihtavuori 500-series very-slow high-energy powder; targets the .300 PRC / 7mm PRC space against Reloder 26 / N570.

name: H110
notes: Spherical magnum-pistol / small-bore-rifle powder. Identical to Winchester 296. The standard heavy-charge magnum revolver and supersonic .300 BLK powder. Critical dual-use entry.

name: Lil'Gun
notes: Designed specifically for .410 bore and .22 Hornet but heavily adopted as a top supersonic .300 Blackout powder. Very fast burn rate for its application class.

name: Power Pro 2000-MR
notes: Alliant Power Pro mid-burn ball powder for medium-rifle cartridges. Less common than TAC/CFE 223 but used by ball-powder enthusiasts in PRS Gas Gun and AR-15 reloads.
